items.title: Chronic vulvar sclerosis and atrophy
items.contentHtml:
Chronic vulvar sclerosis and atrophy

  • Vulvar lichen sclerosus is a chronic inflammatory dermatosis of autoimmune etiology that produces sclerosis (hyalinized collagen thickening) and epidermal atrophy.
  • Histologically, epidermal thinning is observed, hyalinization of dermal collagen and a band lymphocytic infiltrate below the hyaline zone.
  • Progressive sclerosis leads to loss of normal vulvar architecture, with effacement of the folds and narrowing of the introitus.
  • Atrophy causes the skin to become fragile, prone to fissures and ecchymosis.

items.title: Severe vulvar itching and pain
items.contentHtml:
Intense vulvar itching and pain

  • The cardinal symptom is intense vulvar itching, often unbearable, which worsens at night and is accompanied by vulvar pain (dyspareunia, burning).
  • Chronic scratching can induce lichenification (thickening of the skin), making differential diagnosis difficult lichen simplex chronicus.
  • Vulvar pain is usually secondary to fissures, erosions or synechiae; dyspareunia is a common complaint in sexually active women.
  • The intensity of the itching can cause insomnia, anxiety and significant deterioration in quality of life.

items.title: White, atrophic and stiff lesions
items.contentHtml:
White, atrophic and cardboardy lesions

  • Typical lesions are white plaques (leukoplakia), atrophic (thin skin like cigarette paper) and stiffened (wrinkled, parchment-like appearance).
  • The cardboard texture is due to hyalinization of the collagen in the superficial dermis, which gives a firm, parchment-like consistency to the touch.
  • The lesions are usually symmetrical and can extend from the clitoris to the perineum and perianal area, forming a “8” or “hourglass” pattern.
  • In advanced stages, the vulvar mucosa becomes whitish, smooth and shiny, with loss of normal folds.

items.foco: Purpura and fissures on the vulva
items.title: Purpura and fissures on the vulva
items.contentHtml:
Purpura and fissures in the vulva

  • Vulvar purpura (ecchymosis or petechiae) is a key finding, secondary to capillary fragility due to dermal atrophy.
  • Linear fissures, often in the interlabial folds or posterior fork, are very painful and may bleed.
  • The combination of purpura and fissures is highly suggestive of vulvar lichen sclerosus and helps differentiate it from other vulvar dermatoses.
  • Minimal trauma (scratching, sexual intercourse) can cause purpura and fissures, which aggravates pain and pruritus.

items.foco: Synechiae and fusion of labia minora
items.title: Synechiae and fusion of labia minora
items.contentHtml:
Synechiae and fusion of the labia minora

  • synechiae (adhesions) and fusion of the labia minora are sequelae of chronic inflammation and sclerosis.
  • Fusion can obliterate the vaginal introitus, causing stenosis of the introitus and difficulty with urination or sexual intercourse.
  • phimosis of the labia minora may occur. clitoris (adhesion of the clitoral foreskin), which traps secretions and causes pain.
  • These anatomical alterations are irreversible without surgical treatment, but early medical therapy can prevent them.

items.title: Complication: vulvar squamous cell carcinoma
items.contentHtml:
Complication: vulvar squamous cell carcinoma

  • Vulvar lichen sclerosus is a precancerous lesion that confers a 4-7% risk of developing vulvar squamous cell carcinoma.
  • Malignant transformation usually occurs after years of evolution (mean 10-20 years) and can manifest as an ulcer or persistent nodule.
  • Differentiated vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia (differentiated VIN type) is the precursor lesion most associated with lichen sclerosus, unlike the usual VIN related to HPV.
  • In case of any suspicious lesion (ulceration, induration, exophytic growth) a biopsy should be performed to rule out malignancy.

items.title: First-line treatment: topical corticosteroids
items.contentHtml:
First-line treatment: topical corticosteroids

  • First-line treatment is an ultra-potent topical corticosteroid, such as clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment.
  • Typical regimen: apply a pea-sized amount to the affected area once a day at night for 4 weeks, then gradually reduce to maintenance doses (2-3 times per week).
  • Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) are second line, due to their safety profile and lower efficacy.
  • The response to treatment is evaluated by improvement in pruritus and reduction in inflammation; complete remission may require indefinite maintenance therapy.

items.title: Biopsy if malignancy is suspected
items.contentHtml:
Biopsy when malignancy is suspected

  • Vulvar biopsy is indicated for any lesion suspected of malignancy: non-healing ulcer, nodule, hyperkeratotic area, erytration or atypical pigmentation.
  • Biopsy should be performed in 4 mm punch, taking a sample from the edge of the lesion that includes the dermis and epidermis.
  • Histopathology looks for dysplasia or invasive squamous cell carcinoma, and should be evaluated by a dermatopathologist with experience in vulvar pathology.
  • The biopsy also helps confirm the diagnosis of lichen sclerosus when there is doubt clinical, showing the typical findings of hyalinization and atrophy.

items.title: Association with autoimmune diseases
items.contentHtml:
Association with autoimmune diseases

  • Vulvar lichen sclerosus is associated with autoimmune diseases such as Hashimoto's thyroiditis, vitiligo, alopecia areata and pernicious anemia.
  • Up to 30% of patients present antithyroid antibodies (antithyroid peroxidase) or clinical autoimmune thyroid disease.
  • Association with systemic lupus erythematosus and Sjögren's disease has also been described.
  • screening for autoimmune thyroid disease (stimulating hormone) is recommended. thyroid and antibodies) at the time of diagnosis and periodically.

items.foco: Affects vulva, perineum and perianal area
items.title: Affects vulva, perineum and perianal area
items.contentHtml:
It affects the vulva, perineum and perianal area

  • The typical distribution is in vulva, perineum and perianal area, forming a “8” or “hourglass” pattern.
  • The vulva includes the labia majora, labia minora, clitoris and rear fork; the perianal area is affected in approximately 30% of cases.
  • Perianal extension can cause anal pruritus and fissures, being confused with other causes of chronic anal pruritus.
  • The vaginal mucosa is not usually affected, unlike the vulvar and perianal skin.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis with lichen simplex chronicus
items.title: Differential diagnosis with lichen simplex chronicus
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with lichen simplex chronicus

  • Lichen simplex chronicus is a chronic scratching dermatitis that produces lichenification (thickening of the skin) without atrophy or sclerosis.
  • Unlike lichen sclerosus, in lichen simplex chronicus there is no purpura, fissures or white atrophic stiffened lesions.
  • Lichen simplex chronicus is usually secondary to an underlying cause of pruritus (e.g., candidiasis, contact dermatitis), while lichen sclerosus is primarily autoimmune.
  • biopsy is the gold standard: in lichen simplex chronicus there is hyperkeratosis, acanthosis and dermal fibrosis, without hyalinization characteristic of lichen sclerosus.

items.title: Long-term follow-up for neoplastic risk
items.contentHtml:
Long-term follow-up for neoplastic risk

  • Every patient with vulvar lichen sclerosus requires long-term follow-up every 6-12 months, for life, due to the risk of vulvar squamous cell carcinoma.
  • At each visit, careful inspection of the entire vulva and perineum should be performed, documenting any changes in the appearance of the lesions.
  • The patient should be educated about monthly vulvar self-examination and the identification of warning signs (ulcer, nodule, bleeding).
  • Maintenance treatment with topical corticosteroids (for example, clobetasol propionate 0.05% 2-3 times a week) reduces inflammation and may reduce the risk of malignancy.
